speaker: interviewer
text: Don't-- don't-- don't feel bad about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why through the years, I blacked it out. I never told--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. It's going to do you a lot of good. I'm telling you through experience, it's going to do you a lot of good to do what you've done today. It's important.

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean, you did already, too?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were plenty beating, that's for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, don't tell me now. Tell me there, tell me when it-- when it rolls. Tell me about the beating. Tell me how you were treated.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about the fathers having their feet--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I said, we WA-- have to walk to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was about four, five miles. And they didn't allow you to wear the wooden shoes, that you shouldn't wear it out. You should just use that when you do some work for them. So some of the fathers' feet got infected, and they weren't treated. And I seen that gangrene had set in. And they died right on the street. And didn't care. They said they just beat you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who was guarding you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was guarding you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: SS, or just Wehrmacht, or what?

speaker: interviewee
text: SS, Wehrmacht, I don't know from one from the other, you know. They were just German soldiers. So we did that every day for I believe about six weeks. And then they build some tents right across from this benzene refinery, and they put us into those tents. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was terrible conditions. We had no bathing facilities, nothing. Absolutely nothing. We had so much lice. Clothes lice, or I don't know what they call it. You could just take your hands and put it in and take it out like that, you know? Lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lice. I don't know, I think it's clothes lice, or human-- I don't know. And the only way we were able to warm up with everybo-- each other's body. Otherwise, we had no clothing, nothing in those tents. And it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believe it was still the spring, but it was very cold, extremely cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spring of what? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spring of '44. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So it was very bad. And they treated us terrible. Each time you couldn't even have the strength, because you couldn't eat. So if you couldn't move those heavy irons, which caused by the bombs, they would beat you. Some of-- some of, they were beaten to death. And as I said, being across the street from the benzene refinery, we were able to see when they bombed the place. And always day before, they used to come. They used to say that it was the Americans who came. And they would draw a line, right, on the sky-- you know, it's sort of a white line, and it would go down to a point. So we knew exactly where the point was, that's where the bomb going to fell. And we were looking forward to it, that it should be destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lot of those fathers who previously I talked about-- I wish I could remember all the names-- I know that one of them was Mr. Vare. Mr. What?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Vare. Vare.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: V-A-R-E. V-A-R-E, or 'Y' in the end. And this is Mr. Vare who I was telling you about the ghetto before, at the brick factory. They used to own that. And he was there with his son. And he was trying to cuddle his son when he seen the bomb was falling. And his son died his hand. His son was hit by, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in this place, also for [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't remember exact time how long, but it was too long to suffer it, that I know. And then we were taken to a other place. It was, or it must have been in the wintertime of '44, because there was big snow. Or was it '45? '44?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spring we were taken-- '45. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It could have been the-- no, it was in '44, the late, must have been December, or November and December, up to January. So we were there, about 600 people. I also was stationed in one of those horse barn, you know. If you were lucky, they had a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean, like a stable?

speaker: interviewee
text: A stable, yeah. Stable. And if you were lucky, they had a little hay on the floor. If not, just that cold cement. And most of the people couldn't take it. They died. The typhus epidemic came, and a lot of them died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were doing some tunnels in the mountains. You were digging a tunnel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Digging, and also were unpacking some-- in the railroad, the railroad tracks. We were unpacking it. So I was there for about two months or so. And most of the people died. And one day, when we were unpacking the railroad tracks, one of them fell on my legs and broke here, and broke.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the knee? Broke the knee-- the kneecap? And the toes?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my toes. So I thought that that's going to be the end of me, you know. And I didn't have the strength anymore to maneuver. I was just back there, just skin and bone. So they let me stay [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] inside. And then in a couple of days, they put me on a train again. And I went back to Buchenwald. They took me back to Buchenwald. We were on that train for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, packed like sardines. And then for days and days, I don't know how long.

speaker: interviewer
text: All these people that you were in all those different stable, were they all Hungarian, or Czechoslovakian? What kind of nationalities were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were mixed? Well, did you know what kind of nationalities?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Czechoslovakian, Some-- even some Polish, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you stuck with the people that you knew, is that it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, didn't make no difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of language you spoke?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke, then I learned a little bit German. I was able to-- enough that I was able to communicate in German, or Hungarian, you know. Or Czechoslovakian. A little bit of Polish, I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, going back to Buchenwald-- what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So then we were put in that train. And as I said, it was the wintertime. And now it was snowing, and it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] really, we had no food, nothing. And when somebody died, we just took their clothes. We were happy to get their clothes and get a little warm, you know. And by the time I arrived to Buchenwald, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I must have been completely out. I wouldn't say in a coma, but I was unconscious. So I was put together with the dead people from the train who died in this journey. And I was told, which I didn't know, that one of the people who worked in the crematorium, one of the other prisoner that took me down, he seen that I still had some life in me and took me out of the dead people. And I just ended up in one of the barracks, you know. And that's the way I woke up. And I didn't know where I was. That must have been the spring of 1945. Sometime in February, or something like that. Or March.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to call it the infirmatary there. And we stayed there, you know, in those bunk, on top of the other. And-- In a hospital, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hospital, infirmatary, they called--

speaker: interviewer
text: Infirmary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Infirmary. So we stayed there for a while. And I told that I had seen terrible things through those years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I never experienced anything like that. As we were looking out from our bunk, there was another transport come in. And these people came completely naked. They were undressed. And they didn't look people. They were beaten in each other's flesh. Was gobs of flesh was all over on the body.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I asked them-- what they looked like to me, this is the first people who came from outer space or something. So I asked the other people, what happened? What is this all about? What is these people? Where is that coming from? So they told me that they were-- [SIGHS] they were in those trains, the wagons. And they came for days and days without food. And out of all that suffering, they had beaten to each other's flesh. That was, I think, the worst I had seen of all the experience I had had in the concentration camp. So we were there for a while. And then I was able to walk again a little bit. And-- [SIGHS] Were there doctors there to help you? There were any doctors?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if they were doctors, or they were just people, you know. They certainly didn't give me any medication, anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were very happy just to able to have those bunks, to-- even though it was just wood. It was nothing on there. You-- that was like a sanatorium to us. We were very happy that we were able to be there, and we were out of the cold. So I thought that we were privileged to be in that place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: No roll calls?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't give you any roll calls? You didn't have to go on roll calls in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Those people couldn't move, the most of them who was there, you know. I was surprised that they even kept those people, because they were just, like, practically half-dead, most of them. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just enough to-- practically, not to eat enough. When I was liberated, I was about 62 pounds. So, you know. That's why I couldn't go to that death march what they had, when they told the people they all have to come and go. Otherwise, the rest of them, going to be able shoot them, killed, who stays behind. But it didn't matter if they shoot me or not, because I couldn't-- I didn't have the strength to do, to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, as I said, until I was there, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I start to ask again about my brother-in-law. Because I told you that the first instant, when I was in Buchenwald the first time, I had met him. And when I was able to walk a little bit, we were able to go outside. And behind the other barbed wire, I heard the voice who I remember-- it was a Hungarian fellow who used to live in Brussels, Belgium. And he was taken from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew my brother-in-law was a friend of his. And I asked him if he seen my brother-in-law. He says, sure, and he's looking all over for you. So then my brother-in-law came, and he united again, which was unbelievable in a place--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Mashu Reisman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so he took care of me when he first looked at me. He was able to hold me in his hand. He cried. And I thought that he's crazy, what is he crying for? I didn't know. You don't see yourself in that condition. [CRYING] And then we stayed there, because, you know, I-- [CRYING] I was taken to a so-called hospital there.

speaker: interviewer
text: To where?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-called hospital, what they called.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so-called hospital, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-called hospital, but really wasn't a hospital. So anyway, and so my brother-in-law came to visit me, and support. And that was just, I believe, about two months, or six weeks before the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you liberated? You were liberated in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated in Buchenwald, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vaguely, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, I was in such a poor condition that I didn't had strength to really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] joy. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother-in-law with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- you know, you couldn't believe it, that was possible, that you really free. You didn't believe that. Yeah, he was with me. Yeah. And then he told me, he says, it's no point for you to go back to Czechoslovakia, Berehovo, because you don't know if your sister's still alive. He says, why don't you come to Brussels, Belgium. This, she know my sis--

speaker: interviewer
text: To where in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brussels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, to Brussels.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brussels, Belgium. So that's what I did. After the war, I went right to Brussels, Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I checked my card today, I believe it must have been August.

speaker: interviewer
text: In August? What did you do from April to August?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have stayed in Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Buchenwald, mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your brother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life after you were liberated in Buchenwald? How did you recuperate? Can you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have very vague memory of it. I know that we were very anxious to go back. And I was very upset that I haven't heard anything about my sisters, if they are alive or not. So I really have very vague memory after--

speaker: interviewer
text: You got back your strength?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was able to maneuver. I was able to walk. And naturally, gained a few pounds before. I believe it must-- because I checked that card, which, when I arrived to Brussels, Belgium-- I showed you that little card this morning. And I believe that that's what it says, so it must have been in August. Is it possible, August? We were liberated May 2nd.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: April 11th.

speaker: interviewee
text: April 11th? On  the card, it says May 2nd. April 11th. So I would have stayed there from April to August? I don't believe. I don't know. I know that we stayed there for a while, because we were waiting to be transported to Brussels, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the camp during the liberation? What did you do all day long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll be honest with you, that I really don't-- I have very poor recollection after. Because the condition that I was in. And I was just looking forward to leave the whole camp, and leave behind all that experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember seeing soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I seen some American soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when you arrived in Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I met my sister, which I was a great joy to know that she escaped the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she escape the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she went from-- when Belgium was occupied, they went to France, and I think they stayed in Marseilles. And her children was saved by some of the Gentile people there. I mean, she paid them to take care of them at the time. And they were living under false papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of your family survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all survived except my mother. She was killed in Auschwitz. And all my cousins, and all my aunts. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And my sisters survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the younger one, who went to Auschwitz, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the younger one also was in Marseilles. Because, as I said to you, that three of my sisters emigrated to Brussels, Belgium. My youngest sister was only 14 years old. So-- so as I said, from Brussels, when Hitler came in, they all went to Marseilles, and that's where they were liberated. They also lived under false papers there. But then she emigrated, my younger sister emigrated to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Israel. She died in Netanya, very young, about-- about 38 years ago. She was only 33. She left three children, and they raised there. And my sister Elizabeth, who also lived in LA, Los Angeles-- she died here. She was age of 38. She died from myasthenia gravis. It's like part of a multiple paralysis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me your life in Brussels. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brussels was very nice. I was there for about three months, you know. And then we heard that my two sisters is alive-- Elizabeth and Margaret. And we went back to Czechoslovakia-- went back to my hometown. And there was a Belgian general who was a political prisoner, and they met after the war with my two sisters in Germany. And they send a message to my older sister, who was living in Brussels, Belgium. And when this general came back, they gave the message to my sister that Elizabeth and Margaret went back to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I heard that, I went to the Czechoslovakian Council. And I told them that, you know, I want to go back. Because I knew my two sisters that they're not very ambitious people, and they would remain there. And I didn't want them to remain under the Russia. The part where we came from, in '43, when Russia took it over, it became [NON-ENGLISH], which means United Russia. And it's still under the same occupation. So the Czechoslovakian Council advised me not to go back there. Because if you go back in your age, they pick you up on the street and they take you to Siberia. It was through. The Russians, they-- example, if they needed somebody's store or something, they just went in and they threw all your furniture out and took it over. I had learned that when I got back. So I told to the Council, it doesn't matter what. I have to go back, because I want to see my sisters, and I don't want them to remain there. I don't want to live anymore among those people who did this terrible thing to me. So I went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very difficult, because everything was bombed, bombed out. So you have to do a lot of walking. And when you got out of the train, and to change into one country to the other. But I finally made it, and I find my two sisters at home. And that was very true, that under the Russian regime, it was very, very bad at the time. You feared, as I said, if you walk down a street, they picked you up. They took a lot of people into Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were there three days. And I talked my sister into to leave. And we left, and went back to Prague. And we told that-- they said that in Prague, the Russians not going to stay there, they're going to leave. But we stayed there six weeks. And we learned that they're not leaving, they're going to stay there. So we didn't want to stay under the Russians. So we-- they told us that if we go to that little town called Pilsen, where they make that famous Pilsen beer, then we'll be able to go back to Germany and be in a DP camp, and be able to emigrate, either to Israel, or to America. Displaced person camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Displaced person camp. So we stayed there for about six, as I said, for I don't know how many weeks. We stayed in this place in Pilsen, and we waited to go back to Germany. And then we went back, and we were taken to [NON-ENGLISH] in this place called Deggendorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell this, please. You don't know how to spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it on the pa--

speaker: interviewer
text: Say it again. What is the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Deggendorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Deggendorf.

speaker: interviewee
text: Deggendorf. D-E-N-G--

speaker: interviewer
text: D-O-R-F-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: T.

speaker: interviewee
text: --T. Deggendorf. And it was a Hitler Youth house. They were housing Hitler Youths there before the war. So we stayed there in this DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in this displaced person camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was under the-- It was managed under the American, the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: But no, the people who were in the camp, like you, who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh the camp? Yeah, we have displaced people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but they're what nationality?

speaker: interviewee
text: About thousands of us from every nationality-- Germans. Matter of fact, on a picture, the little picture there, there is one lady who I met there who used to be one of the biggest banker in Berlin. Dr. Panis was the name. Her son was-- their friend was the Rothschilds.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was in the displaced persons camp, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was in this displaced person camp, yeah. Her husband was killed. And I'm there in the picture with her. She sent her son before, way before. When the bad time came in Germany, she sent them to England, was raised by the Rothschild family in England. And she was looking forward to go back to England. There was a lot of interesting people in the camp after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay? And we stayed there from '45 to 1946. The 31st of August, I came to the United States, went through [NON-ENGLISH]. He's something that all the displaced person was able to come into the country without waiting for a quota. They had the priority. Because you had families here? Is that why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because I had family here.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll go, continue the next tape. You're doing fine.
